region.name: Pike County
region.bioregion: Northeastern Pennsylvania · Pocono Plateau · Delaware River Highlands

regionalIntelligenceProfile.dominantForest: Northern hardwoods with oak-hickory on dry ridges; hemlock–northern hardwood ravines along Delaware tributaries
regionalIntelligenceProfile.elevationNote: County mean ~1,200 ft; Delaware River valley ~400 ft to plateau balds ~1,800 ft
regionalIntelligenceProfile.phenologyStage: Late spring transition — ephemerals may be fading, mountain laurel may be opening, morels may be ending below 1,200 ft

recentRainfall.summary: Thursday rain elevated creek levels; ravines muddy, rocky ridges drying fast

platformScope.label: Pike County Preview
platformScope.detail: Using Pike County, Pennsylvania as the current regional preview. Locations outside this bundle may show nearest editorial content — not county-specific outdoor intelligence.

platformMission.lead: A regional outdoor intelligence dashboard and field laboratory — designed for ethical outdoor learning.
platformMission.pillars: Learn about the natural world · Get outdoors · Build toward research-grade observations
platformMission.honest: Observation capture is in development. Fieldry will become the structured observation ledger aligned with the Waypoint Observation Standard.

thisWeekOutdoors.title: Valley fog, laurel buds, and the last morels
thisWeekOutdoors.summary: Editorial preview: morels may be closing below 1,200 feet. Mountain laurel may be opening on north-facing ledges. Afternoon thunderstorms may build after 3 p.m. — plan morning field time.
thisWeekOutdoors.heroAlt: Mist lifting from the Delaware River valley at dawn, Pike County

weather.conditions: Humid · valley fog at dawn · thunderstorm chance Saturday after 3 p.m.

happeningNow: Mountain laurel buds may be swelling on cool north slopes — watch for first blooms this weekend
Yellow morels may be fading below 1,200 ft; check ash–elm edges after rain
Black bear activity may increase near campgrounds — secure attractants
Late warblers may still be moving through Delaware Water Gap canopy
Trail mud possible in ravines; ridge tops may be drying fast after Thursday rain
thisWeekOutdoors.weekdayFocus: Read the species spotlight and teacher's lab. Note three spring ephemerals still in bloom on your commute walk.
thisWeekOutdoors.weekendPrompt: Complete the Delaware River ravine investigation — fog line, birdsong, and one photograph from a fixed bearing.

blocks.label: Weather

blocks.label: Phenology

blocks.label: Wildlife

regionalFieldNotes.title: Morel season winding down below 1,200 ft
regionalFieldNotes.body: Yellow morels (Morchella americana) have been reported in old apple orchards near Matamoras and along ash–elm edges in Dingman Township — editorial field note, not a verified survey. Above 1,400 ft on the plateau: likely mostly done. Ethics reminder: cut at soil line; leave small specimens; never trespass.
regionalFieldNotes.tryThis: Walk one south-facing slope and one north-facing slope — note where soil moisture lingers.
supportingFacts: Morels often associate with dying elm, ash, and apple — not every year on the same calendar week
Editorial note only — not a verified fruiting survey
regionalFieldNotes.citizenScience: Optional phenology note — no harvest location required.

regionalFieldNotes.title: Black bears on the move — secure attractants
regionalFieldNotes.body: PA Game Commission has reported increased bear sightings in Lackawaxen and Shohola townships (editorial summary — confirm with official PA sources). Cubs may be visible with sows near riparian corridors. Remove feeders at night; store trash in bear-resistant containers.
regionalFieldNotes.tryThis: From a safe distance, note one sign of bear activity (scat, claw mark, overturned log) without approaching.
supportingFacts: Increased sightings often follow spring green-up near riparian corridors
Confirm current advisories with PA Game Commission before visiting
regionalFieldNotes.citizenScience: Report only through official PA channels if required — never precise home locations.

regionalFieldNotes.title: Pennsylvania warbler migration tail
regionalFieldNotes.body: Late migrants may still be possible along the Delaware Water Gap — Blackburnian and magnolia warblers in canopy oaks. Breeding birds may be establishing territories: ovenbird, red-eyed vireo, wood thrush.
regionalFieldNotes.tryThis: Five-minute sound map at dawn: mark three distinct songs.
regionalFieldNotes.citizenScience: Optional eBird checklist — location obscured to hotspot.

regionalFieldNotes.title: Trail conditions after rain
regionalFieldNotes.body: Hornbecks Creek and Adams Creek trails muddy with standing water in low sections. Ridge trails (Milford Knob, Cliff Park) drying. Creek crossings running higher than last week.
regionalFieldNotes.tryThis: If you turn back because of mud, write why — that is good field judgment.

seasonalWatch.outdoorChallenge: Pick one watch species and photograph it from the same bearing twice this week.

watchCards.name: Mountain laurel
watchCards.whyMatters: Laurel bloom is a visible phenology anchor — when buds open on north slopes, spring is handing off to early summer in the Poconos.
watchCards.watchFor: Pink-tinged buds swelling on cool north-facing ledges; compare bud stage on north vs. south aspects of the same hill.
watchCards.ecology: Evergreen shrub of acidic oak–hemlock forest; flowers support native bees; dense thickets provide cover for songbirds.
watchCards.weatherLink: Cool nights and recent rain may slow bloom on north slopes — warm south aspects may open first.
watchCards.observeEthically: Photograph from trail; do not pick blooms on public land. Protected in some park boundaries.

watchCards.name: Yellow morel
watchCards.whyMatters: Morels mark the end of the low-elevation spring fungi window — a lesson in timing, tree partners, and soil moisture.
watchCards.watchFor: Ash–elm edges and old orchard ground below 1,200 ft after rain; fruiting bodies may be sparse — look for habitat, not guarantees.
watchCards.ecology: Mycorrhizal and saprotrophic roles near dying hardwoods; part of forest nutrient cycling — not just a harvest target.
watchCards.weatherLink: Warm days dried south ridges while valley fog kept low terraces moist — one of the last reasonable weeks below 1,200 ft.
watchCards.observeEthically: Identify with confidence; never eat uncertain fungi. Cut at soil line; leave small specimens; never share exact locations publicly.

watchCards.name: Spring ephemerals
watchCards.whyMatters: Ephemeral wildflowers vanish quickly — documenting their fade teaches how forest floor light changes through May.
watchCards.watchFor: Yellowing trout lily leaves, trillium petal drop, and uncurling fern fiddleheads in hemlock ravines.
watchCards.ecology: Spring ephemerals capture early-season light before canopy closure; they feed pollinators and enrich soil when senescing.
watchCards.weatherLink: Canopy closure and warming soils on south aspects accelerate fade; cool ravines may hold flowers a few days longer.
watchCards.observeEthically: Observe and photograph only — do not collect plants from wild populations.

seasonalWatch.citizenScience: Optional phenology phase score — anonymous plot ID.
seasonalWatch.subscriptionValue: Subscribers: full phenology calendar + historical comparison.

weekendFieldInvestigation.title: Delaware River ravine — fog line and birdsong
weekendFieldInvestigation.drivingQuestion: How does the valley fog line change sound, light, and species activity between dawn and 10 a.m.?
weekendFieldInvestigation.when: Saturday or Sunday morning — start 30 minutes before sunrise
materials: Camera or phone for one evidence photo
weekendFieldInvestigation.place: Public trail along Delaware River — Cliff Park, Milford Beach boardwalk, or DWGNRA river access (your choice)
steps: BEFORE YOU GO: Check thunderstorm forecast — leave ridge if storms approach. Bear-aware food storage.
OBSERVE (15 min): Sit at one spot. List 5 seen, 3 heard, 1 smelled. Note fog height relative to river.
ASK: What question does the fog raise about temperature and sound?
PHOTOGRAPH: Wide frame showing fog line + one detail (leaf, bird, rock) with date and time in notes.
CONNECT: Name two links between water, slope, and what you heard.
REFLECT: What did you expect before you arrived?
RETURN: Repeat same bearing next month for phenology comparison.
weekendFieldInvestigation.citizenScience: Optional fog + birdsong checklist — county rounded, anonymous.

researchBrief.title: Earlier leaf-out on south-facing Appalachian slopes
researchBrief.summary: Some regional studies suggest south-facing plots may green 8–12 days before north-facing plots at equal elevation — relevant when timing morel, laurel, and blueberry observations in the Poconos.
researchBrief.localApplication: In Pike County, compare a south-facing Lackawaxen hillside with a north-facing ravine near Dingman Falls this weekend. Score leaf-out 1–5 on the same tree species if possible.
researchBrief.uncertainty: Regional studies vary by elevation and aspect; this brief summarizes patterns — not site-specific predictions for your exact trail.
researchBrief.source: Placeholder — Northeast regional phenology network summary
researchBrief.subscriptionValue: Full citation list + educator discussion guide.

featuredVideo.title: Reading valley fog along the Delaware
featuredVideo.summary: Show fog pooling at the river before explaining inversion basics. Ends with: find your local fog line Saturday morning.

photoEssay.title: Eight frames: spring ephemerals fading in hemlock ravine
photoEssay.summary: A walk through Dingman Glen — trout lily leaves yellowing, trillium petals dropping, fern fiddleheads uncurling. Evidence of the handoff from spring to early summer.

frames.caption: White trillium — petal drop on forest floor

photoEssay.outdoorChallenge: Return to same ravine in three weeks — one photograph.

rotation.disclosure: Species chosen for this week's Pike County field guide. Timing reflects seasonal education — not live detection, harvest reports, or automated rotation.

entries.title: Buds opening on cool slopes — peak bloom spreading uphill
entries.timing: Late May through June · earlier on south aspects, later above 1,200 ft
entries.habitat: Acidic rocky slopes and ravines · oak–hemlock forest · often north-facing ledges
entries.whereToLook: North-facing ledges and ravines at 800–1,400 ft; Delaware Water Gap north slopes; Cliff Park trail margins
entries.whyThisWeek: Pink-tinged buds may be visible on north slopes and ravines now — a useful phenology teaching anchor; compare bloom stage week to week.
entries.ecologicalRole: Foundation shrub in acidic forests; evergreen cover for wildlife; flowers support native pollinators in late spring.
observationTips: Compare bud stage on north vs. south aspects of the same hill
Photograph leaf vein arc from trail — do not pick blooms
Note associated trees: oak, hemlock, mountain laurel often co-occur on ledges
identification: Leaves alternate, oval, yellow vein arc on inner edge
Flowers in terminal clusters — pink buds open to white cups
Often on acidic, rocky slopes with oak and hemlock
entries.lookAlikes: Sheep laurel (Kalmia angustifolia) — narrower leaves, smaller flowers, often in wetlands
entries.conservationNote: Native throughout Pennsylvania. Do not pick blooms on public land — photograph from trail. Protected in some park boundaries.

image.alt: Mountain laurel buds on a north-facing ledge, Pike County
image.caption: Buds opening on cool slopes — field photograph placeholder

entries.outdoorChallenge: Find laurel on a north-facing slope vs. south-facing — note bud stage difference.
entries.citizenScience: Optional bloom stage photo — plot anonymous.
entries.subscriptionValue: Full FGDS species page + look-alike guide.

entries.title: Last calls in the valleys
entries.timing: Late April – late May · ending below 1,200 ft
entries.habitat: River terraces, old apple ground, ash–elm edges after rain
entries.whereToLook: Low terraces along the Delaware and Lackawaxen below 1,200 ft; old orchard edges near Matamoras
entries.whyThisWeek: Queued for rotation — morel season is closing on warm south slopes; check shaded edges after rain while learning identification.
identification: Cap honeycombed; attached to stem at base
Hollow through cap and stem when sliced lengthwise
Often near dying elm, ash, apple, or tulip poplar
entries.conservationNote: Cut at soil line; leave small specimens; never trespass; never share exact harvest spots publicly.

teachersNotebook.title: Lab: Spring ephemeral transect (50 minutes outdoors)
objectives: Score leaf-out or bloom stage along a 50 m transect
Relate aspect to phenology using field evidence
Record metadata: date, weather, slope direction
safety: Stay on school grounds or approved park
procedure: 10 min intro indoors — driving question only
30 min field — pairs walk transect, record every 10 m
10 min reflect — one sentence per pair: what surprised us?
teachersNotebook.outdoorChallenge: Homework: one photograph from home window with three-line journal entry.
teachersNotebook.citizenScience: Class may opt in to regional phenology network — parental permission required.
teachersNotebook.subscriptionValue: Educator tier: PA standards alignment + printable worksheet.

conservationUpdate.title: DWGNRA trail maintenance and streambank stabilization
conservationUpdate.summary: National Park Service crews working on erosion control along Delaware River access paths near Milford. Temporary detours possible weekends. Project protects brook trout habitat and reduces sediment after flood events.
conservationUpdate.habitat: Delaware River riparian zone — brook trout nursery streams and floodplain forest
conservationUpdate.stewardship: Erosion control reduces sediment loading after flood events; staying on marked paths protects recovering banks
conservationUpdate.lands: Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area (public)
conservationUpdate.whatYouCanDo: Stay on marked paths; volunteer trail days posted through Friends of DWGNRA.
conservationUpdate.outdoorChallenge: Visit a public viewpoint; note one sign of erosion control or recovery.
conservationUpdate.citizenScience: Stream watch volunteers needed — optional, training provided.

featuredProject.name: ForageCast
featuredProject.headline: Understand why species appear where and when they do.
featuredProject.summary: Habitat literacy, seasonal timing, and ethical field investigation for Pike County Preview — educational estimates, not guarantees. Learn conditions first; confirm outdoors.

experiences.name: Waypoint Studio Dashboard
experiences.mission: What is happening outdoors this week? What can you learn, observe, and investigate — safely and ethically?
experiences.whatItDoes: Surfaces regional outdoor intelligence — weather context, phenology, wildlife notes, and field investigations.
experiences.whoItHelps: Teachers, hikers, naturalists, and curious residents starting their week outside.
experiences.futurePlanned: Progressive learning paths, optional observation capture, and additional honest county bundles.
experiences.educationalValue: The regional trailhead: outdoor intelligence, lessons, and stewardship for Pike County Preview.

experiences.name: ForageCast
experiences.mission: Understand why species appear where and when they do — habitat bands, seasonal timing, and ethical investigation.
experiences.whatItDoes: Teaches habitat literacy and seasonal timing through schematic terrain bands and species education.
experiences.whoItHelps: Foragers, botanists, and field investigators learning to read place and season — not harvest scoreboards.
experiences.futurePlanned: Expanded species library, phenology overlays, and WOS-linked foray notes in Fieldry.
experiences.educationalValue: Read timing and terrain like a field guide. Prediction zones are educational estimates, not harvest guarantees.

experiences.name: Fieldry
experiences.mission: The structured observation ledger — a private field notebook building toward research-grade environmental records.
experiences.whatItDoes: Captures WOS-aligned field observations locally — date, place, habitat, weather context, confidence, and ethical notes.
experiences.whoItHelps: Anyone building careful observation habits for personal learning and optional future citizen science.
experiences.futurePlanned: Photo attachment, Darwin Core export UI, and opt-in anonymous county-level contribution.
experiences.educationalValue: Date, place, habitat, weather, notes, confidence, and privacy — designed to support WOS-aligned evidence over time.

experiences.name: Waypoint Scenes
experiences.mission: Creative reflection from field evidence — honest photography, memory, and visual storytelling after time outside.
experiences.whatItDoes: Organizes field photographs into collections and gentle motion studies — evidence from a place, not performance.
experiences.whoItHelps: Wildlife photographers and memory-keepers who want to honor frames from time outside.
experiences.futurePlanned: Deeper metadata linking frames to Fieldry observations and regional galleries.
experiences.educationalValue: Treat each frame as evidence from a place. Motion honors the photograph; it does not replace observation.
